content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob play a game with NNN piles of stones where the ithi^{th}ith pile has AiA_iAi​ stones initially.

They take alternate turns starting with Alice.

On Alice's turn, she will:


Choose indices iii and j (1≤i,j≤N)j \ (1 \le i, j \le N)j (1≤i,j≤N) where iii and jjj have the **same** parity.
Remove 111 stone each from piles iii and jjj.

Note that both piles must have **at least** one stone before performing the move.

The piles iii and jjj can also be **same**, resulting in removal of two stones from a single pile if it has **more than one** stone.


On Bob's turn, he will:


Choose indices iii and j (1≤i,j≤N)j \ (1 \le i, j \le N)j (1≤i,j≤N) where iii and jjj have **different parity**.
Move 111 stone from pile iii to pile jjj.

Note that pile iii must have **at least** one stone before performing the move.


The first player who is unable to make a move loses. Determine the winner of the game if both players play optimally.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN — the number of piles.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers AiA_iAi​, denoting number of stones on ithi^{th}ith pile.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line `Alice` if Alice wins; otherwise, print `Bob`.


Note that you may print each character in uppercase or lowercase. For example, the strings `BOB`, `bob`, `Bob`, and `BoB` are all considered identical.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 10510^5105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Consider the following sequence of moves:

Alice chooses i=1i=1i=1, j=3j=3j=3 on her move and removes one stone from each pile. Thus, AAA becomes [1,1,0][1,1,0][1,1,0]
Bob chooses i=2i=2i=2, j=3j=3j=3 and moves one stone. Thus, AAA becomes [1,0,1][1,0,1][1,0,1]. He can choose other combination of piles but it can be shown that it won't change the final result.
Alice chooses i=1i=1i=1, j=3j=3j=3 and removes one stone each from both piles.

Since Bob has no valid move left, Alice wins.
Test case 222: Consider the following sequence of moves:

Alice chooses i=1i=1i=1, j=3j=3j=3 on her move and removes one stone from each. Thus, AAA becomes [2,1,1][2,1,1][2,1,1]
Bob chooses i=3i=3i=3, j=2j=2j=2 and moves one stone. Thus, AAA becomes [2,2,0][2,2,0][2,2,0].
Alice chooses i=j=1i=j=1i=j=1 and removes two stones. Thus, AAA becomes [0,2,0][0,2,0][0,2,0]
Bob choose i=2i=2i=2, j=1j=1j=1 and moves one stone. Thus, AAA becomes [1,1,0][1,1,0][1,1,0].

Alice cannot make a move and loses.
Test case 333: Consider the following sequence of moves:

Alice chooses i=j=1i=j=1i=j=1 and removes two stones. Thus, AAA becomes [0,2][0,2][0,2]
Bob chooses i=2i=2i=2, j=1j=1j=1 and moves one stone. Thus, AAA becomes [1,1][1,1][1,1].

Alice cannot make a move and loses.
